Are these rational fears? I don’t even know anymore. 

But when I see tech CEOs on stage asking, rhetorically, “can you imagine raising a child without chatgpt?” My blood curdles.

I’m not a super technical person and I’ve never built and shipped a piece of software that people use… but it seems like AI usage is eroding the fabric of shared knowledge and existence that gets passed from person to person, generation to generation, and makes us human. That’s the scary part to me.

For me, it’s the phenomena of “large company lost the one guy who knew how this all works” potentially impacting the entire internet that scares me to death.

At a certain point there are too many layers of abstraction for us to realistically problem solve anymore….

What might actually work? Probably combinations: inoculation + debunking together, proactive platform design changes, and possibly stronger regulation that goes well beyond labelling.

Warnings alone are unlikely to be sufficient. Regulators need to catch up with the science. 🔬

11/12
Across all three papers, the same pattern: transparency — simply telling people they're being manipulated — reduces AI-driven influence at best partially, and often not at all.
This is a critical problem, because the EU AI Act relies heavily on transparency as its main tool.

Personality-targeted ads were significantly more persuasive than non-targeted ads in all three studies.

The popup warning had no practically meaningful effect. Equivalence tests confirmed the popup's impact was statistically indistinguishable from zero.


10/10

“My eyes are up here,” he said, amused when Reiner sputtered and lifted his gaze instantly. 

“Sorry,” Reiner said, cheeks flushed. Jean cocked his head, regarding Reiner for a moment, before his eyes fell on the book title. _Wake Me Up Inside._ It was one of the books Jean hadn’t got around to read yet, and he wondered why Reiner had stopped his reading to ask him to shift. Did it have something to do with the book? He was so lost in his thoughts that he nearly missed Reiner’s gaze lowering again. He cleared his throat to catch Reiner’s attention. 

“What?” Jean snapped, losing patience quickly. 

“I was just wondering–“ Reiner trailed off, and Jean tapped his foot impatiently. Apparently, it was enough because Reiner seemed to find his words again. “Do you knot?”

it's easy to not use AI to write. The closest I come is when I search for history shit and the AI response comes up first, but I know enough about history that I've personally noticed SO much misinformation in those AI results that there's no use case for it anyhow.

value.text:
I keep seeing "all authors use AI, the real issue is they're being forced to be sneaky about it" headlines.

Full stop No. This is a lie peddled for normalization. It's schoolyard-level pressure tactics "C'mon, all the cool kids are already doing it. We won't tell. It's fine. We're on your side."

images.alt: After speaking publicly about her use of Claude, Sun received criticism from people who were offended by the notion that AI could replace a human editor. Critics argued that AI can’t transform your ideas or challenge you as much as a human. Sun says she found the comments confusing. Most Substackers can’t afford to hire a human editor, so by adding Claude and instructing it to challenge her, Sun argues it’s made her process more rigorous. “To me, it is kind of like using Grammarly. You have a tool that says, ‘this sentence is bad,’ and then I have to go fix it,” she says. “[Claude is] more willing to tell me this entire section is bad and you should cut it. It's like a higher level of thinking and abstraction than Grammarly can do.”
